title: श्री नीलकण्ठेश स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
नीलकण्ठं नमामीशं विषधारिणमव्ययम्।
समुद्रमन्थनोद्भूतं हालाहलधरं शिवम्॥१॥

देवानां रक्षणार्थाय जगतां हितकाम्यया।
विषं सन्धारयामास कण्ठे यो लोकशंकरः॥२॥

कालकूटविषं घोरं सर्वलोकभयङ्करम्।
कण्ठे धृत्वा कृपासिन्धुं नीलकण्ठं नमाम्यहम्॥३॥

देवासुरैस्तदा मन्थे समुद्रे क्षीरसागरे।
हालाहलं समुत्पन्नं जगद्दाहकरं विषम्॥४॥

त्रस्ता देवाश्च गन्धर्वा ऋषयो मुनयस्तथा।
शरणं जग्मुरीशानं विषनाशाय शंकरम्॥५॥

पार्वत्या सहितः शम्भुः करुणार्द्रेण चेतसा।
गृहीत्वा पाणिना विषं पपौ कण्ठे धृतं तदा॥६॥

नैव मृत्युर्न चालस्यं विषं कण्ठे स्थितं यतः।
नीलवर्णं बभूवाथ कण्ठं तस्य महात्मनः॥७॥

नीलकण्ठ महादेव कृपासागर शंकर।
त्राहि मां सर्वपापेभ्यो विषदुःखाच्च रक्ष माम्॥८॥

यः पठेत् प्रातरुत्थाय नीलकण्ठस्तवं शुभम्।
विषबाधा न तस्यास्ति सर्वरोगविनाशनम्॥

॥ इति श्री नीलकण्ठेश स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
Nilakantham Namamiisham Vishadharinam Avyayam.
Samudramanthanodbhutam Halahaladharam Shivam.||1||

Devanam Rakshanarthaya Jagatam Hitakamyaya.
Visham Sandharayamasa Kanthe Yo Lokashankarah.||2||

Kalakutavisham Ghoram Sarvalokabhayankaram.
Kanthe Dhritva Kripasindhum Nilakantham Namamyaham.||3||

Devasurais Tada Manthe Samudre Kshirasagare.
Halahalam Samutpannam Jagaddahakaram Visham.||4||

Trasta Devashcha Gandharva Rishayo Munayastatha.
Sharanam Jagmur Ishanam Vishanashaya Shankaram.||5||

Parvatya Sahitah Shambhuh Karunardrena Chetasa.
Grihitva Panina Visham Papau Kanthe Dhritam Tada.||6||

Naiva Mrityur Na Chalasyam Visham Kanthe Sthitam Yatah.
Nilavarnam Babhuvatha Kantham Tasya Mahatmanah.||7||

Nilakantha Mahadeva Kripasagara Shankara.
Trahi Mam Sarvapapebhyo Vishaduhkhachcha Raksha Mam.||8||

Yah Pathet Pratar-Utthaya Nilakanthastavam Shubham.
Vishabadha Na Tasyasti Sarvarogavinashanam.

|| Iti Shri Neelakanthesha Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
विष धारण करने वाले, अव्यय, समुद्र मंथन से उत्पन्न हालाहल को धारण करने वाले नीलकण्ठ ईश शिव को मैं नमन करता हूँ।

देवताओं की रक्षा और जगत की हित कामना से जिन्होंने विष को कण्ठ में धारण किया, उन लोकशंकर को नमस्कार।

सर्व लोकों को भयभीत करने वाले घोर कालकूट विष को कण्ठ में धारण करने वाले कृपासिन्धु नीलकण्ठ को मैं नमस्कार करता हूँ।

जब देवासुरों ने क्षीरसागर का मंथन किया तब जगत को दग्ध करने वाला हालाहल विष उत्पन्न हुआ।

भयभीत देव, गन्धर्व, ऋषि और मुनि विष-नाश के लिए ईशान शंकर की शरण में गए।

पार्वती सहित शम्भु ने करुणार्द्र हृदय से हाथ में विष लेकर पान किया और कण्ठ में धारण किया।

न मृत्यु हुई न आलस्य — विष कण्ठ में स्थित रहा, जिससे उस महात्मा का कण्ठ नीलवर्ण हो गया।

हे नीलकण्ठ महादेव, कृपासागर शंकर! सर्व पापों और विषजन्य दुःखों से मेरी रक्षा करें।

जो प्रातःकाल उठकर नीलकण्ठ स्तव का पाठ करता है, उसे विषबाधा नहीं होती और सर्व रोगों का नाश होता है।

viniyog.shloka: अस्य श्रीनीलकण्ठेशस्तोत्रमन्त्रस्य मार्कण्डेय ऋषिः। अनुष्टुप् छन्दः। श्रीनीलकण्ठशिव देवता। ॐ नमः शिवाय बीजम्। विषनाशिनीशक्तिः। सर्वरोगनिवारणं कीलकम्। श्रीशिवप्रीत्यर्थे पाठे विनियोगः॥

benefits: Traditionally invoked for protection from all forms of poison and toxicity
Traditionally recited for removal of diseases and grants health
Traditionally recited for removal of all sins through shiva's infinite compassion
Teaches the supreme sacrifice of Lord Shiva for the world
Powerful remedy for Nag Panchami and Maha Shivaratri
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Neelakanthesha Stotram (श्री नीलकण्ठेश स्तोत्रम्) stands as a devotional hymn of praise. This revered text originates from Shiva Purana (Samudra Manthana Katha). Devotees recite this prayer to destroy all sins through Shiva's infinite compassion and protect from all forms of poison and toxicity. It is also said to grant fearlessness and divine protection. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Nag Panchami. The text contains 8 verses and takes around 5 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Neelakanthesha Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Neelakanthesha Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Prayer to Shiva as Neelakantha, who drank poison to save the world. For health and protection.

seoFaqs.question: Why is Shiva called Neelakantha in this stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: Neelakantha literally means 'the blue-throated one' (neela = blue, kantha = throat). The name commemorates the Samudra Manthana episode narrated in the Bhagavata Purana (Canto 8), the Mahabharata, and the Vishnu Purana, in which the gods and demons churned the Cosmic Ocean and the deadly halahala poison emerged first. To save creation, Shiva drank the poison; Parvati pressed his throat to prevent it descending further, and the poison stained his neck a permanent blue. The Neelakanthesha Stotram is a hymn of gratitude for this supreme act of compassion. (Source: Bhagavata Purana 8.7; JKYog India.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the scriptural source of the Neelakanthesha Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses praising Shiva as Neelakantha appear across multiple texts. The Shiva Purana includes hymns celebrating his role as Vishapaharaka, the destroyer of poison. The Bhagavata Purana's Samudra Manthana narrative supplies the theological foundation, while the Skanda Purana includes specific Neelakantha stutis recited at pilgrimage sites such as Neelkanth Mahadev near Rishikesh and Mandi in Himachal Pradesh. Multiple regional ashtakams and pancharatnams bearing the name Neelakantha have been compiled by acharyas over centuries. (Source: Shiva Purana; Bhagavata Purana 8.7; Skanda Purana sthala-mahatmyas.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does reciting the Neelakanthesha Stotram offer?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional commentaries state that devotees who chant this stotra are protected from visha (poison) in all its forms: literal toxins, betrayal by trusted persons, snake-bite, planetary afflictions (especially Rahu-Ketu and Saturn), and the venom of negative thoughts. Because Shiva willingly accepted what no other deity could endure, the Neelakanth form is invoked for emotional resilience and the capacity to absorb suffering without being destroyed by it. The stotra is also chanted before snake-related rituals during Nag Panchami. (Source: Shiva Purana; Sawan Mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the most auspicious time to chant this stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The entire Shravan month (July to August), and especially the Mondays of Shravan, is the peak season for Neelakantha worship, since the Samudra Manthana is traditionally believed to have occurred during this period. Pradosha evenings, Mahashivaratri, and Nag Panchami are also prescribed times. Devotees travelling to Neelkanth Mahadev temple near Rishikesh, where Shiva is said to have rested after drinking the halahala, recite the stotra during the trek to the shrine. (Source: Drik Panchang Shravan calendar; Neelkanth Mahadev temple traditions.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Neelakantha story teach a spiritual lesson?
seoFaqs.answer: Commentators on the Bhagavata read the halahala episode as a metaphor for the spiritual seeker's path. When the mind is churned in sustained sadhana, latent negativities, fears, and karmic poisons surface first, before the nectar of self-realisation. The seeker must, like Shiva, neither swallow the poison fully (suppression) nor spit it out (projection on others) but hold it consciously in the throat (vishuddha chakra) where speech and emotion are purified. Reciting the Neelakanthesha Stotram is therefore considered a meditation on transmutation. (Source: Glorious Hinduism; Exotic India Art article on Samudra Manthan.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the connection between Neelkanth and the Mrityunjaya mantra?
seoFaqs.answer: Both invocations address Shiva as the conqueror of death. The Mahamrityunjaya mantra (Rig Veda 7.59.12) calls upon Tryambaka who liberates from death like a ripe cucumber from its vine, while the Neelakantha form embodies that same conquest in narrative form: by absorbing the world-ending poison, Shiva proved that he stands beyond death itself. Devotees often pair the Neelakanthesha Stotram with Mahamrityunjaya japa during illness, surgery, or grief, treating them as complementary expressions of Shiva's grace as Vishapaharaka. (Source: Rig Veda 7.59.12; Shiva Purana.)
